Spec Reference: spec section Part 2 - Delete PredictLiveIndices and Its Consumers.

title: Regression Assertion: No Predicted-Vs-Live WARN Under Non-Zero Base-Index
description:
Problem: Task 2-1 deletes the source of the misleading predicted=...__0.0 live=...__X.Y WARN, but spec AC #4 demands a runtime guarantee that the diagnostic is gone, not silenced. Without an automated regression assertion, a future re-introduction of any predicted-vs-live diagnostic could resurface the WARN under non-zero base-index configs.

Solution: Add a regression sub-test (or extend existing base-index drift sub-test) in cmd/bootstrap/reboot_roundtrip_test.go that runs the bootstrap-and-restore round-trip with non-zero base-index and pane-base-index on the test tmux server, then reads <PORTAL_STATE_DIR>/portal.log and asserts zero lines match the regex `predicted=.*__\d+\.\d+ live=.*__\d+\.\d+`. The test must use the isolated internal/tmuxtest socket fixture, and must use a session name without a leading dash.

Outcome: A new (or extended) _test.go test under cmd/bootstrap/ runs under the integration build tag, drives the bootstrap orchestrator against an isolated tmuxtest socket with non-zero base indices, completes a full restore + hydrate cycle, and fails if portal.log contains any line matching the predicted-vs-live regex. Plus a plain (non-integration) unit test verifies the regex itself matches the offending shape and ignores the preserved armPanes:202 warning.

Do:
- Open cmd/bootstrap/reboot_roundtrip_test.go. Decide whether to extend TestPhase5RebootRoundTripBaseIndexDrift with the regex assertion, or add a sibling test (e.g. TestPhase5RebootRoundTripNoPredictedVsLiveWarn). Prefer extending if it can be threaded in without disturbing the existing shape.
- After bootstrap completes, resolve the path to portal.log via PORTAL_STATE_DIR.
- Read the entire portal.log file. Compile regex `regexp.MustCompile(\`predicted=.*__\\d+\\.\\d+ live=.*__\\d+\\.\\d+\`)`. Iterate lines; fail with t.Fatalf if any line matches.
- Use a session name without a leading dash (alpha/beta from existing fixture is fine).
- Use only the isolated tmuxtest socket fixture; do NOT touch the developer's primary tmux server.
- Run go test -tags=integration ./cmd/bootstrap/... and confirm the assertion passes.
- Verify the regex is meaningful and false-positive-safe with a unit test (in the same _test.go file or a sibling cmd/bootstrap/predicted_vs_live_regex_test.go) named TestPredictedVsLiveRegex_MatchesOffendingShapeAndIgnoresArmPanesWarning. The test compiles the same regex and asserts:
  - It matches a representative offending line: WARN | restore | session "alpha": pane 0 predicted=alpha__0.0 live=alpha__1.1.
  - It does NOT match the preserved armPanes:202 shape: WARN | restore | session "alpha": live pane count 2 != saved count 3.
  This unit test is plain go test ./cmd/bootstrap/... (no integration build tag) so it runs on every CI invocation without requiring tmux.

Acceptance Criteria:
- A test in cmd/bootstrap/reboot_roundtrip_test.go runs under integration build tag with non-zero base-index and pane-base-index.
- After bootstrap and hydrate complete, the test reads <PORTAL_STATE_DIR>/portal.log and asserts zero lines match `predicted=.*__\d+\.\d+ live=.*__\d+\.\d+`.
- The test uses only the isolated internal/tmuxtest socket fixture.
- The test uses a session name without a leading dash.
- The regex is anchored enough to avoid false positives on unrelated log lines.
- The assertion runs AFTER bootstrap orchestrator has completed and hydrate has been driven.
- go test -tags=integration ./cmd/bootstrap/... passes.
- A unit test (TestPredictedVsLiveRegex_MatchesOffendingShapeAndIgnoresArmPanesWarning) compiles the same regex and proves it (a) matches a representative predicted=...__0.0 live=...__1.1 line and (b) does not match the preserved armPanes:202 "live pane count != saved count" shape.
- No t.Parallel() call added.

Tests:
- Integration assertion: portal.log contains zero predicted-vs-live WARN lines under non-zero base-index.
- Unit test (TestPredictedVsLiveRegex_MatchesOffendingShapeAndIgnoresArmPanesWarning): regex matches offending shape and not armPanes:202 warning.
- Existing base-index drift round-trip behaviour unchanged.
- No developer-primary-server interaction.

Edge Cases:
- Isolated tmux socket only: must use tmuxtest.New(...) fixture; must not invoke tmux kill-server against any other socket.
- Session name without leading dash: keeps test orthogonal to Phase 1 task 1-3.
- Regex false-positive avoidance: pattern requires both `predicted=...__N.N` and `live=...__N.N` segments. Verified by the unit test rather than manual injection.
- Assertion timing: must run after bootstrap and hydrate complete.

Spec Reference: spec sections Acceptance Criteria item 4 and Testing Requirements item 2.

title: Analysis (Cycle 1): Local Cleanups From Implementation Analysis
description:
Address findings from Analysis (Cycle 1).

Six approved local-cleanup tasks identified during implementation analysis: deduplicate the canonical hydrationTriggerEvents slice, extract a shared portal.log scan helper, promote applyBaseIndices to a shared test helper, tighten AC #2 unit test to cover the FIFO byte-write clause, collapse RegisterPortalHooks/RegisterPortalHooksWithLogger into a single entry point, and unexport MigrateHydrationHooks to seal it inside RegisterPortalHooks.

title: Export Hydration Trigger Events And Eliminate Two Test-Side Shadows
description:
**Problem**: The canonical hydrationTriggerEvents slice ("client-attached", "client-session-changed") is hand-rolled in three places: production source internal/tmux/hooks_register.go:26-29, in-package external-test mirror expectedHydrationTriggerEvents at internal/tmux/hooks_register_test.go:28-31, and cross-package integration mirror leadingDashHydrationTriggerEvents at cmd/bootstrap/reboot_roundtrip_test.go:1144-1147. Spec § Fix Scope explicitly anticipates extension ("If the slice is later extended, the migration scan must follow it"). On extension, production updates while both test mirrors silently under-cover.

**Solution**: Export the canonical slice from internal/tmux; have both test files consume the exported symbol directly.

**Outcome**: One canonical list. Adding a new event in production automatically widens coverage in both migration tests and the cross-package round-trip integration test.

**Do**:
1. In internal/tmux/hooks_register.go, expose the canonical slice — rename hydrationTriggerEvents to exported HydrationTriggerEvents (var []string), or expose func HydrationTriggerEvents() []string returning a defensive copy. Prefer the var form unless mutation drift is a concern.
2. Update all in-package iteration sites (hooks_register.go, migration code) to the exported name.
3. Delete expectedHydrationTriggerEvents at internal/tmux/hooks_register_test.go:28-31; replace its 8 iteration sites in hooks_migration_test.go with the exported symbol.
4. Delete leadingDashHydrationTriggerEvents at cmd/bootstrap/reboot_roundtrip_test.go:1144-1147; update verifyHydrationHookEntries (line 1347) and any other call sites in that file. bootstrap_test already imports tmux.
5. Remove the obsolete justification docstring at cmd/bootstrap/reboot_roundtrip_test.go:1137-1143.

**Acceptance Criteria**:
- The literal ["client-attached", "client-session-changed"] appears exactly once in the repo.
- grep -rn 'expectedHydrationTriggerEvents\|leadingDashHydrationTriggerEvents' . returns no matches.
- Both hooks_migration_test.go and reboot_roundtrip_test.go reference the exported symbol from internal/tmux.
- All existing tests pass.

**Tests**:
- go test ./internal/tmux/...
- go test -tags=integration ./cmd/bootstrap/...

**Spec Reference**: § Fix Scope (extension-anticipation clause).

title: Extract Shared Portal.log Scan Helper For Assertion Reuse
description:
**Problem**: cmd/bootstrap/reboot_roundtrip_test.go contains verifyNoPredictedVsLiveWarns (lines 443-458) and verifyNoHydrateTimeoutWarns (lines 1375-1397) sharing ~12 lines of byte-identical file-IO scaffolding: open portal.log, return on os.IsNotExist, split on "\n", iterate lines, fail on match. Only the per-line predicate differs (regex vs. dual substring).

**Solution**: Extract a single helper taking a predicate and per-AC failure message; rewrite both existing helpers as one-line wrappers.

**Outcome**: One implementation of the portal.log scan plumbing; future AC assertions add a one-line wrapper instead of duplicating IO scaffolding.

**Do**:
1. Add func assertNoLogLineMatches(t *testing.T, logPath string, pred func(string) bool, failFmt string, args ...any) near the existing helpers.
2. Rewrite verifyNoPredictedVsLiveWarns (lines 443-458) as a one-line wrapper.
3. Rewrite verifyNoHydrateTimeoutWarns (lines 1375-1397) as a one-line wrapper.
4. Preserve each helper's distinct diagnostic message exactly.

**Acceptance Criteria**:
- Both pre-existing helpers retain public names and call signatures.
- File-IO + ENOENT-tolerance + line-iteration plumbing appears exactly once.
- Distinct failure-message strings remain byte-identical to today.

**Tests**:
- go test ./cmd/bootstrap/...

title: Promote ApplyBaseIndices To A Shared Test Helper
description:
**Problem**: The new applyBaseIndices helper at cmd/bootstrap/reboot_roundtrip_test.go:513-519 issues the canonical four-call set-option -g/-s × base-index/pane-base-index sequence. The pre-existing TestPhase3Integration_RestoreUsesLiveIndicesUnderBaseIndexDrift performs the same four calls inline at internal/restore/integration_test.go:325-328 with hard-coded "1".

**Solution**: Promote applyBaseIndices to a shared test-only package (internal/tmuxtest preferred — both call sites already use tmuxtest.Socket).

**Outcome**: One implementation of the base-index configuration sequence shared across all integration-tag tests.

**Do**:
1. Move applyBaseIndices from cmd/bootstrap/reboot_roundtrip_test.go:513-519 to internal/tmuxtest. Suggested signature: func ApplyBaseIndices(t *testing.T, ts *Socket, base, paneBase int).
2. Update the round-trip caller in reboot_roundtrip_test.go to tmuxtest.ApplyBaseIndices(t, ts, base, paneBase).
3. Replace the inline block at internal/restore/integration_test.go:325-328 with tmuxtest.ApplyBaseIndices(t, ts, 1, 1).

**Acceptance Criteria**:
- applyBaseIndices defined exactly once.
- The four-call sequence appears verbatim in exactly one helper body.
- Both caller files consume the helper.

**Tests**:
- go test -tags=integration ./internal/restore/...
- go test -tags=integration ./cmd/bootstrap/...

title: Tighten AC #2 Unit Test To Cover FIFO Byte-Write Clause
description:
**Problem**: Spec § AC #2 requires the cobra-level argv parse test to "drive the cobra command tree via Execute() against a leading-dash positional argument and assert exit 0 + FIFO byte written". TestStateSignalHydrate_AcceptsLeadingDashSessionViaCobraExecute at cmd/state_signal_hydrate_test.go:622-647 installs a no-op replacement for signalHydrateRunFunc that captures cfg.Session and asserts exit 0 + captured session, but the seam short-circuits runSignalHydrate before any FIFO is opened.

**Solution**: Tighten the unit test to install a stub FIFO seam and assert a byte was written, preserving the existing seam pattern. Fallback: explicit cross-reference comment if seam stubbing requires production-code changes outside scope.

**Outcome**: AC #2 satisfied verbatim by a single unit test.

**Do**:
1. In cmd/state_signal_hydrate_test.go:622-647, identify the FIFO open/write seam used by runSignalHydrate.
2. Replace the no-op signalHydrateRunFunc swap with the real runSignalHydrate plus a stub FIFO recording bytes written.
3. Drive cobra via Execute() with the leading-dash positional argv; assert exit 0, captured session equals the leading-dash value, and the stub FIFO received the expected payload.
4. Restore the seam via t.Cleanup() per project DI pattern.
5. Fallback only if seam stubbing requires production-code seam additions outside this fix's scope: add an explicit cross-reference comment pointing to TestRebootRoundTrip_LeadingDashSessionName.

**Acceptance Criteria**:
- Unit test drives Execute() with leading-dash session argv.
- Asserts exit 0 AND that a byte was written through the FIFO seam (or fallback comment).
- No production-code seam additions outside the existing *Deps / package-level-var pattern.
- Test does not use t.Parallel().

**Tests**:
- go test ./cmd -run TestStateSignalHydrate_AcceptsLeadingDashSessionViaCobraExecute
- TestRebootRoundTrip_LeadingDashSessionName continues to pass unchanged.

**Spec Reference**: § AC #2.

title: Collapse RegisterPortalHooks/RegisterPortalHooksWithLogger Into A Single Entry Point
description:
**Problem**: internal/tmux/hooks_register.go:233-282 exposes RegisterPortalHooks(c) and RegisterPortalHooksWithLogger(c, log). The only production caller (bootstrapadapter.HookRegistrar at internal/bootstrapadapter/adapters.go:68-70) already has a logger. The no-logger form is exercised only by hooks_register_test.go. Future production code could route through the no-logger form and silently lose eviction visibility.

**Solution**: Collapse into a single RegisterPortalHooks(c *Client, log MigrationLogger) error tolerating nil.

**Outcome**: One name, one shape, no risk of future production code routing through a logger-less path.

**Do**:
1. In internal/tmux/hooks_register.go, delete RegisterPortalHooksWithLogger; rename its body to RegisterPortalHooks(c *Client, log MigrationLogger) error. Keep the nil guard.
2. Update internal/bootstrapadapter/adapters.go:68-70 (HookRegistrar) to call the unified RegisterPortalHooks(c, log).
3. Update test call sites in internal/tmux/hooks_register_test.go to pass nil or a recording logger.
4. Update doc comments — drop "stable external-caller surface" language.

**Acceptance Criteria**:
- RegisterPortalHooksWithLogger no longer exists.
- RegisterPortalHooks takes *Client and MigrationLogger.
- All production and test call sites compile and pass.
- Passing nil for log falls through to the noop logger.

**Tests**:
- go test ./internal/tmux/..., go test ./internal/bootstrapadapter/..., go test ./cmd/bootstrap/... all pass.

title: Unexport MigrateHydrationHooks To Seal It Inside RegisterPortalHooks
description:
**Problem**: MigrateHydrationHooks at internal/tmux/hooks_register.go:189-227 is exported; its doc pins it as bootstrap-internal. Non-production callers are three tests at internal/tmux/hooks_migration_test.go:346,395,441 exercising the migration in isolation. The export creates a second valid entry point for "hook installation" — a future contributor could invoke it standalone and end up with no hydration hooks registered.

**Solution**: Unexport to migrateHydrationHooks; refactor the three tests to drive RegisterPortalHooks with a recording logger and MockCommander.

**Outcome**: Migration is structurally sealed inside RegisterPortalHooks.

**Do**:
1. Rename MigrateHydrationHooks to migrateHydrationHooks. Update its single in-package call site.
2. Refactor the three tests to drive RegisterPortalHooks (or RegisterPortalHooksWithLogger if Task 5 not landed) with a recording logger and MockCommander. Arrange MockCommander so the migration phase exhibits each test's condition; assert the same observable outcome.
3. Confirm via repo-wide grep that no caller outside internal/tmux references MigrateHydrationHooks.

**Acceptance Criteria**:
- MigrateHydrationHooks (capital M) no longer exists.
- migrateHydrationHooks is called from exactly one site.
- The three migration tests still cover their original conditions through RegisterPortalHooks.
- All tests pass.

**Tests**:
- go test ./internal/tmux/...
- grep -rn 'MigrateHydrationHooks' . returns no matches.

title: Session Scrollback Preview
Phase 1: Read pipeline and structural enumeration foundations
Tail-N reverse-scan helper happy path
description:
Problem: Preview's read pipeline must return at most the last N newline-terminated records from a .bin scrollback file in sub-millisecond time regardless of total file size. No existing helper performs a bounded tail read; daemon code reads whole files via os.ReadFile, which would balloon to ~3.7MB allocations per focus event for busy sessions and put the synchronous-in-Update decision out of budget.

Solution: Add a new helper in internal/state (alongside ScrollbackFile) that opens the target path, seeks to the end via os.File + Seek(0, io.SeekEnd), reads backwards in fixed-size chunks against a single held file descriptor, counts \n bytes until N records are accumulated (or the file is exhausted), and returns the trailing terminated bytes. Signature: func TailScrollback(path string, n int) ([]byte, error). Happy-path scope only — no-content and error-shape branches are tightened by tasks 1-2 and 1-3.

Outcome: A callable helper exists in internal/state that, given a .bin file with at least N newline-terminated lines, returns exactly the last N lines as raw bytes (preserving trailing \n). Cost is decoupled from total file size.

Do:
- Create helper in new file under internal/state/ (e.g. scrollback_tail.go); name and exact filename at implementer discretion. Must live alongside ScrollbackFile.
- Signature: func TailScrollback(path string, n int) ([]byte, error). Must be exported and callable from internal/tui's production adapter for ScrollbackReader.Tail.
- Open file once with os.Open; defer close. Do NOT close-and-reopen between chunk reads — single-fd invariant.
- Use Seek(0, io.SeekEnd) for size; if zero return (nil, nil) — fully validated in task 1-2.
- Choose chunk size constant (e.g. 8 KiB or 64 KiB) and read backwards; maintain cursor; seek back by min(chunkSize, cursor); read; logically prepend; count \n bytes; repeat until N+1 newlines seen or cursor reaches 0.
- Slice from byte after (N+1)th-from-last \n to last \n inclusive — all returned content fully terminated.
- If file has fewer than N terminated lines, return everything from start-of-file up to and including the last \n.
- Return (bytes, nil) for any successful read with at least one terminated line; defer trailing-partial and zero-line shapes to task 1-2.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Helper exported from internal/state and resolvable from internal/tui adapter (no circular imports).
- Given file with K terminated lines where K > N, returns last N lines as single []byte including final \n.
- Given file with K < N, returns all available terminated lines without padding, error, or (nil, nil).
- Given file whose last N lines span multiple chunks, output is byte-identical to naive whole-file tail -n N.
- Single os.File held across all chunk reads (no f.Close() in loop body).
- Happy-path return is always (non-nil bytes, nil error).

Tests:
- 'it returns the last N terminated lines from a file with more than N lines' — fixture: 1500 lines, N=1000.
- 'it returns all lines when file has fewer than N' — fixture: 5 lines, N=1000.
- 'it returns exactly N lines when file has exactly N lines' — boundary.
- 'it correctly assembles tail when N lines span chunk boundaries' — (N+1)th-from-last newline at least two chunk-sizes from EOF.
- 'it returns content with the trailing newline preserved'.
- 'it holds a single file descriptor across the reverse scan' — assert via test seam (e.g. openFunc) that exactly one open per call. Omit if no clean seam reasonable.

Edge Cases:
- File with fewer than N lines returns all lines (not (nil, nil)).
- File with exactly N lines returns all N (no off-by-one).
- Tail spanning multiple chunk boundaries assembles correctly (chunk stride is implementation detail).

Spec Reference: .workflows/session-scrollback-preview/specification/session-scrollback-preview/specification.md § History Depth > Read Pipeline, § Cross-cutting Seams > State Package API Reuse, § Architecture Summary > Test seams > ScrollbackReader > Return contract.

title: Tail-N helper no-content shape
description:
Problem: The preview placeholder ('(no saved content)') is gated on the helper returning (nil, nil) for ENOENT, zero-byte files, and files containing only an unterminated partial line. Without these branches the call site in internal/tui cannot distinguish 'file missing' from 'OS error' from 'file present with content', and the placeholder vs error string branching collapses.

Solution: Extend the helper from task 1-1 so all 'no content available' outcomes converge on (nil, nil): ENOENT on open, zero-byte file size, and any reverse scan that finds zero \n bytes. When the file has terminated lines plus a trailing partial fragment, the partial fragment must be excluded from the returned tail.

Outcome: Three 'no content' cases (ENOENT, zero bytes, zero terminated lines) all return (nil, nil). A file with K terminated lines plus a trailing partial returns only the K terminated lines as (bytes, nil), never including the partial fragment.

Do:
- In the helper from task 1-1, when os.Open returns an error, check errors.Is(err, fs.ErrNotExist); on match, return (nil, nil). Other open errors flow through to task 1-3's branch.
- After Seek(0, io.SeekEnd), if size == 0, return (nil, nil) immediately (no reverse scan).
- During reverse scan, if cursor reaches 0 and total newline count is 0, return (nil, nil).
- Ensure the slice end-point is the last \n byte (inclusive), not EOF — guarantees trailing partial bytes are excluded.
- For a file with K terminated lines plus trailing partial, return all K lines as (bytes, nil).

Acceptance Criteria:
- Tail('/does/not/exist', 1000) returns (nil, nil), no error wrapping ENOENT.
- Zero-byte file returns (nil, nil).
- File containing only 'partial line without newline' returns (nil, nil).
- File containing 'line1\nline2\npartial' returns []byte('line1\nline2\n') and nil error.
- File containing only '\n' (single empty terminated line) returns []byte('\n') and nil — terminated empty line is content.
- No path through no-content branches returns a non-nil error.

Tests:
- 'it returns (nil, nil) for a missing file'.
- 'it returns (nil, nil) for a zero-byte file'.
- 'it returns (nil, nil) for a file with only an unterminated partial line'.
- 'it excludes a trailing partial line from the returned tail'.
- 'it preserves a single empty terminated line as content'.
- 'it does not surface ENOENT as an error' — assert error is literally nil, not an fs.ErrNotExist-wrapping value.

Edge Cases:
- ENOENT vs zero-byte must both produce (nil, nil) — observationally identical at call site.
- File with only unterminated partial line returns (nil, nil) — counts as zero terminated lines.
- File with terminated lines plus trailing partial returns only terminated lines.
- Single bare \n is one terminated line (not zero) and is returned as content.

Spec Reference: .workflows/session-scrollback-preview/specification/session-scrollback-preview/specification.md § History Depth > Read Pipeline > Trailing-newline edge case, § Read-Failure Handling > Placeholder, § Architecture Summary > Test seams > ScrollbackReader > Return contract.

title: Tail-N helper OS-error shape
description:
Problem: OS-level read errors (EACCES, EIO, etc.) must surface as (nil, err) so the call site in internal/tui renders the dedicated error string instead of the placeholder. Conflating these with the (nil, nil) no-content shape would silently hide legitimate failures behind the placeholder, making preview indistinguishable from 'empty file' when the file is unreadable.

Solution: Extend the helper from tasks 1-1 and 1-2 so any open error other than fs.ErrNotExist, and any read or seek error encountered mid-scan, propagates as (nil, err). ENOENT must remain on the (nil, nil) branch from task 1-2 — this task explicitly does NOT reroute it.

Outcome: Permission-denied opens, mid-scan I/O failures, and seek failures all return (nil, non-nil error). ENOENT continues to return (nil, nil). The error wraps the underlying OS error so the caller can debug-log it without per-errno branching at the helper layer.

Do:
- In the helper, when os.Open returns an error that is NOT errors.Is(err, fs.ErrNotExist), return (nil, fmt.Errorf('tail scrollback %s: %w', path, err)) (or equivalent — must use %w so errors.Is works).
- During reverse scan, if any Seek or Read returns a non-nil error other than io.EOF at expected boundary, return (nil, fmt.Errorf('tail scrollback %s: %w', path, err)).
- Do NOT attempt retries — single read attempt per focus event with no per-pane error cache.
- Ensure deferred Close() from task 1-1 still executes on error path.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Open error that is not ENOENT returns (nil, non-nil err) with errors.Is(err, originalOSError) true via %w wrapping.
- Permission-denied file (mode 0000 or chown to another user) returns (nil, non-nil err).
- Read error encountered mid-scan returns (nil, non-nil err) and does not leak partial buffer.
- ENOENT continues to return (nil, nil) — task 1-3 does not change task 1-2's behaviour.
- File descriptor is closed even on error path.

Tests:
- 'it returns an error for a permission-denied file' — os.Chmod(path, 0o000); skip on Windows; t.Skip if running as root.
- 'it wraps the underlying OS error so errors.Is works' — assert errors.Is(returnedErr, fs.ErrPermission) on perm-denied.
- 'it preserves the (nil, nil) shape for ENOENT — does not take the error branch'.
- 'it returns an error from a mid-scan read failure' — inject a failing reader via test seam. Optional: omit if no clean seam.
- 'it closes the file descriptor on the error path' — verify via instrumentation seam from task 1-1, or omit if no clean seam.

Edge Cases:
- Permission-denied open returns error, not placeholder.
- Mid-scan read error returns error and does not leak partial buffer.
- ENOENT does NOT take the error branch — remains on (nil, nil) branch from task 1-2.
- Error wrapping uses %w so callers can errors.Is against fs.ErrPermission etc. without string matching.

Spec Reference: .workflows/session-scrollback-preview/specification/session-scrollback-preview/specification.md § Read-Failure Handling, § Architecture Summary > Test seams > ScrollbackReader > Return contract.

title: Tail-N performance benchmark
description:
Problem: Preview's synchronous-in-Update decision rests on the tail-N read staying fast (p99 < 5ms on a 4 MB representative .bin). Without an automated benchmark guarding this budget, a future change (chunk size regression, accidental whole-file read, allocator-heavy refactor) could silently push read time into 10s of milliseconds, making preview's keystroke handling perceptibly laggy. The spec explicitly requires this benchmark.

Solution: Add a Go benchmark (BenchmarkTailScrollback) under internal/state that generates a 4 MB fixture file (~50k lines of mixed-width content with ANSI escape sequences), resets timer after fixture generation, and runs the tail-N helper with N=1000 in the measured region. A separate _test.go regression-guard test runs the helper on the same fixture and asserts wall-clock duration < 5ms with a t.Skip env opt-out for slow CI runners.

Outcome: A benchmark exists and runs via go test -bench=. ./internal/state/. A regression-guard test fails when 4 MB tail-N read exceeds budget under representative load. Fixture-generation cost excluded from measured region.

Do:
- In same package as helper (likely internal/state), create scrollback_tail_bench_test.go (or merge into existing tail-N test file).
- Write BenchmarkTailScrollback(b *testing.B):
  1. Build 4 MB fixture in b.TempDir() containing ~50k lines, each ~80 chars wide, with synthetic ANSI escape sequences (e.g. \x1b[31mred\x1b[0m) at random intervals.
  2. b.ResetTimer() after fixture generation.
  3. In for i := 0; i < b.N; i++ loop, call helper with N=1000 against fixture; consume result with _ = bytes to prevent dead-code elimination.
- Write TestTailScrollback_PerformanceBudget(t *testing.T):
  1. Generate same 4 MB fixture in t.TempDir().
  2. Call helper once to warm page cache.
  3. Time second call with time.Now() / time.Since.
  4. Assert elapsed < 5 ms.
  5. Include t.Skip('performance test skipped: $PORTAL_SKIP_PERF=1') guard or similar so test can opt out on slow CI.
- Document budget in comments on both, citing spec section.

Acceptance Criteria:
- 'go test -bench=BenchmarkTailScrollback ./internal/state/' runs without error and reports per-op times.
- b.ResetTimer() invoked after fixture generation; reported per-op time reflects only helper's work.
- TestTailScrollback_PerformanceBudget passes on developer machine with time.Since(start) < 5*time.Millisecond for 4 MB fixture after warmup.
- Regression-guard test skips cleanly when PORTAL_SKIP_PERF (or equivalent env opt-out) is set.
- Fixture content includes ANSI escape sequences and varied line widths so benchmark is representative of real tmux capture-pane -e output.

Tests:
- 'BenchmarkTailScrollback runs and reports a per-op time' — meta-assertion.
- 'it reads tail-N from a 4 MB fixture in under 5ms after warmup' — regression-guard test.
- 'the benchmark excludes fixture generation from the measured region' — code inspection: b.ResetTimer() appears after fixture build.

Edge Cases:
- Fixture generation cost must be excluded — b.ResetTimer() after build, not before.
- Regression-guard runs helper twice (warm, then measure) so disk-cache warmth is controlled.
- 5ms budget is for warmed-cache reads (realistic case during cycling); cold-cache reads out of scope.

Spec Reference: .workflows/session-scrollback-preview/specification/session-scrollback-preview/specification.md § History Depth > Read Pipeline > Performance budget.

title: Window-grouped pane enumeration on tmux.Client
description:
Problem: Preview's chrome (window M of N, pane X of Y, window name) and structural cycling (], [, Tab) need a single call returning all windows-with-panes for a session, ordered by window_index then pane_index, with each window's name attached. No existing tmux.Client method composes this — ListPanesInSession returns flat panes without window-name correlation, and there is no list-windows wrapper. The spec prefers a single new read-only method on tmux.Client.

Solution: Add a new method to tmux.Client (e.g. ListWindowsAndPanesInSession(session string) ([]WindowGroup, error)) that runs 'tmux list-panes -t <session> -s -F "#{window_index}|#{window_name}|#{pane_index}"', parses the pipe-delimited output, and groups results by window_index into a []WindowGroup shape exposing both per-window name and ordered pane indices. Tests inject a mock Commander to return canned stdout fixtures — no real tmux server required.

Outcome: New ListWindowsAndPanesInSession(session) method exists on tmux.Client and is callable from any TUI adapter. Returned []WindowGroup is sorted by window_index ascending; each group's panes sorted by pane_index ascending. Window name captured per group. Happy-path scope only — failure modes and empty-result handling are tightened by task 1-6.

Do:
- Define WindowGroup struct in internal/tmux/. Suggested shape: { WindowIndex int; WindowName string; PaneIndices []int }. Exact field set at implementer discretion; callers must be able to read WindowName, count windows (chrome 'Window M of N'), count panes per window ('Pane X of Y'), and read raw indices for state.SanitizePaneKey.
- Add method on tmux.Client: func (c *Client) ListWindowsAndPanesInSession(session string) ([]WindowGroup, error).
- Use c.cmd.Run (trim variant) — match ListPanesInSession pattern.
- Build command: tmux list-panes -s -t <session> -F '#{window_index}|#{window_name}|#{pane_index}'. The -s flag emits all panes across all windows.
- Parse line-by-line; split on '|' with 3 fields expected.
- Group by window_index (preserving first-seen window name); build []WindowGroup sorted by window_index ascending; within each group sort PaneIndices ascending.
- Pipe-in-window-name edge case: tmux allows | in window names. Implementer chooses (i) non-printable separator like \x1f (US — unit separator) — preferred for robustness, OR (ii) strings.SplitN(line, '|', 3) accepting that | in first/second position breaks parsing while later | is harmless because pane_index is numeric. Document choice in method comment.
- Tests use existing Commander mock pattern from internal/tmux/.

Acceptance Criteria:
- ListWindowsAndPanesInSession(session) returns ([]WindowGroup, error) and is exported from internal/tmux.
- Session with windows [0, 1, 2] each having panes [0, 1] returns 3 WindowGroups in order, each with two pane indices in order.
- Non-contiguous window_index values (e.g. [0, 2, 5]) returns slice length 3 with WindowIndex fields [0, 2, 5] in order. Slice does NOT pad gaps — chrome computes 1-based ordinals from slice length, not raw indices.
- Under base-index 1 / pane-base-index 1, returned slice preserves raw indices verbatim.
- Window names containing whitespace ('my window') returned intact.
- Window names containing chosen delimiter character handled per documented strategy.
- Multiple panes per window grouped into correct WindowGroup and sorted ascending by pane_index.
- Method uses c.cmd.Run — no direct os/exec.Command.
- No changes to tmux.Client.CapturePane or any existing capture wrapper. Diff is purely additive.

Tests:
- 'it returns window-grouped panes ordered by window_index then pane_index'.
- 'it preserves non-contiguous window_index values verbatim'.
- 'it preserves base-index 1 raw values'.
- 'it preserves window names containing whitespace'.
- 'it preserves window names containing the pipe delimiter' — fixture per chosen strategy.
- 'it groups multiple panes within the same window correctly'.
- 'it uses the cmd.Run interface'.

Edge Cases:
- Non-contiguous window_index returns slice without padding gaps.
- Base-index 1 / pane-base-index 1 returns raw values verbatim.
- Window names containing | require non-printable separator OR documented SplitN parsing.
- Window names containing whitespace pass through unchanged.
- Multiple panes per window grouped and sorted.

Spec Reference: .workflows/session-scrollback-preview/specification/session-scrollback-preview/specification.md § Multi-pane Rendering Shape > Concrete enumeration call, § Multi-pane Rendering Shape > Chrome Floor, § Architecture Summary.

title: Enumeration failure and empty-result handling
description:
Problem: Preview's open path treats two outcomes identically — 'tmux call failed' and 'tmux call succeeded but returned zero windows or zero panes' — by silently aborting the open. The new ListWindowsAndPanesInSession method must surface both shapes correctly: a non-nil error for tmux non-zero exit, and an empty []WindowGroup slice (with nil error) for empty stdout. Conflating them would force preview to second-guess errors versus content shape.

Solution: Pin the failure and empty-result contract on the method from task 1-5. Any non-zero exit from tmux list-panes is wrapped and returned as a non-nil error. Empty stdout (zero lines) returns an empty []WindowGroup{} slice with nil error. The call site in internal/tui treats both as 'do not open preview' per the spec, but the method preserves the distinction for callers.

Outcome: When tmux returns an error, method returns (nil, non-nil err). When tmux returns successfully but stdout is empty, method returns ([]WindowGroup{}, nil) — usable empty slice, not nil. No new capture wrapper is introduced (re-asserts diff stays additive on listing side only).

Do:
- In method from task 1-5, when c.cmd.Run returns non-nil error, wrap with session name: return nil, fmt.Errorf('list windows and panes for session %s: %w', session, err). Use %w so callers can errors.Is.
- When c.cmd.Run returns ('', nil) (empty stdout, success), return ([]WindowGroup{}, nil) explicitly, not (nil, nil). Empty-but-non-nil slice signals 'session exists but has no panes/windows' cleanly.
- When stdout has only whitespace (e.g. trailing \n from tmux), parse as zero lines and return ([]WindowGroup{}, nil).
- Add code-comment assertion (or CI-time grep test) that diff to internal/tmux/ adds ONLY the new ListWindowsAndPanesInSession method and WindowGroup type — no changes to CapturePane, CapturePane* variants, or any other existing method. Guard against scope creep into capture wrappers.

Acceptance Criteria:
- When underlying cmd.Run returns error, method returns (nil, non-nil err) and error wraps original via %w.
- When cmd.Run returns ('', nil) (empty stdout, success), method returns ([]WindowGroup{}, nil) — empty but non-nil slice, nil error.
- When cmd.Run returns ('\n', nil) or only-whitespace, method returns ([]WindowGroup{}, nil).
- Error message includes session name for debuggability.
- No tmux.Client.CapturePane* method signature is modified by this task or task 1-5; verifiable via git diff review on internal/tmux/.
- Callers can use errors.Is(err, anySentinel) against returned error if cmd.Run exposes sentinels.

Tests:
- 'it returns an error when tmux exits non-zero' — Commander mock returns ('', errors.New('exit status 1: no such session')); assert nil bytes, non-nil wrapping err.
- 'it returns an empty slice when stdout is empty and exit is zero' — mock returns ('', nil); assert non-nil slice, length 0, nil err.
- 'it returns an empty slice for whitespace-only stdout' — mock returns ('\n', nil); assert non-nil slice, length 0, nil err.
- 'the wrapped error includes the session name' — assert strings.Contains(err.Error(), sessionName).
- 'the wrapped error preserves the original via errors.Is' — define sentinel; mock returns it; assert errors.Is(returnedErr, sentinel).
- 'the diff to internal/tmux/ adds no capture wrappers' — code review-level check.

Edge Cases:
- Tmux exit non-zero (session disappeared between Space and call) → (nil, err).
- Empty stdout vs error must be distinguishable.
- Whitespace-only stdout treated as empty (zero lines).
- No new capture wrapper introduced.

Spec Reference: .workflows/session-scrollback-preview/specification/session-scrollback-preview/specification.md § Multi-pane Rendering Shape > Chrome Floor > Enumeration failure handling, § Refresh Semantics > Read Trigger Events > Initial-open ordering, § Source of Preview Bytes, § Architecture Summary.

title: Phase 2: Preview page entry, dismiss, and single-pane content rendering
Define TmuxEnumerator and ScrollbackReader seam interfaces in internal/tui
description:
Problem: Preview's logic in internal/tui must depend on small interfaces (not concrete *tmux.Client or filesystem helpers) so unit tests can run hermetically without a real tmux server, without tmuxtest, and with t.Parallel() safe. The spec mandates two seams (TmuxEnumerator, ScrollbackReader) with a precise three-shape return contract on the reader.

Solution: Add two new dependency interfaces in the internal/tui package — one for window-grouped pane enumeration, one for tail-N scrollback reads keyed by paneKey — together with a record-type alias for the enumeration return shape. The interfaces are pure declarations in this task; production adapters and consumers land in later tasks.

Outcome: internal/tui exposes TmuxEnumerator and ScrollbackReader interface types whose method shapes match the spec exactly, with stateDir intentionally absent from ScrollbackReader.Tail (closed over at construction time). Tests can compile against the interface types using minimal mock structs.

Do:
- In internal/tui (new file, e.g. internal/tui/preview_seams.go), declare:
  - type TmuxEnumerator interface { ListWindowsAndPanesInSession(session string) ([]tmux.WindowGroup, error) } — return type reuses tmux.WindowGroup from Phase 1.
  - type ScrollbackReader interface { Tail(paneKey string) ([]byte, error) }.
- Add doc comment on ScrollbackReader.Tail documenting three observable shapes verbatim per spec:
  - (bytes != nil, nil) — normal content; caller renders bytes verbatim.
  - (nil, nil) — 'no content available' (collapses ENOENT, zero-byte file, and zero-line file with only an unterminated partial). Caller renders the placeholder '(no saved content)'.
  - (nil, err != nil) — OS-level read failure (EACCES, EIO, etc.). Caller renders the error string.
- Document on the interface that stateDir is intentionally hidden (closed over at construction) so tests can mock by paneKey alone.
- Do NOT add adapter wiring in this task (lands in 2-7). Do NOT add a previewModel (lands in 2-2).
- Do NOT introduce a package-level previewDeps variable; spec mandates constructor injection.

Acceptance Criteria:
- TmuxEnumerator and ScrollbackReader are exported types in internal/tui.
- TmuxEnumerator.ListWindowsAndPanesInSession(session string) ([]tmux.WindowGroup, error) matches Phase 1 client method signature exactly.
- ScrollbackReader.Tail(paneKey string) ([]byte, error) has no stateDir parameter.
- Doc comment on Tail enumerates the three return shapes verbatim.
- Package compiles (go build ./internal/tui/...).
- No package-level mutable seam variable exists for preview.

Tests:
- 'it declares TmuxEnumerator with the Phase 1 client signature' — compile-time assertion via var _ TmuxEnumerator = (*tmux.Client)(nil) (or equivalent) in a test file.
- 'it declares ScrollbackReader hiding stateDir behind the interface' — compile-time assertion: a tiny mock implementing only Tail(paneKey string) ([]byte, error) satisfies the interface.
- 'it allows mocks to express the three Tail return shapes' — instantiate three mocks returning (bytes, nil), (nil, nil), and (nil, err); confirm each compiles against the interface.

Edge Cases:
- The stateDir hiding is the load-bearing test seam: any future change adding a stateDir parameter to Tail breaks the construction-time-closure invariant.
- The tmux.WindowGroup type comes from Phase 1; do not redefine it locally.

Spec Reference: .workflows/session-scrollback-preview/specification/session-scrollback-preview/specification.md § Architecture Summary > Test seams, § Cross-cutting Seams > State Package API Reuse.

title: previewModel constructor with injected seams and initial-open flow
description:
Problem: A preview model is needed that owns the embedded bubbles/viewport, current focus indices, captured structural enumeration, and minimal keymap — constructed fresh on every Space press with no caching. The initial-open flow has spec-mandated ordering: enumeration first, fail/empty → silent no-open, success → set focus to window 0 / pane 0 and synchronously read tail-N bytes for that pane, then render chrome and viewport atomically in the first frame.

Solution: Add a previewModel struct in a new file internal/tui/pagepreview.go plus an exported constructor NewPreviewModel(session string, enumerator TmuxEnumerator, reader ScrollbackReader, width, height int) (previewModel, bool) returning (model, ok) where ok=false signals 'do not transition to pagePreview'. The constructor performs steps 1-4 of the spec's initial-open ordering inline; the caller (Sessions page) checks ok to decide whether to switch pages.

Outcome: A constructor-injected previewModel exists that, on construction, runs structural enumeration, returns ok=false on enumeration error or empty result (zero windows, or first window having zero panes), and on success sets focus to (0, 0), reads the focused pane's tail-N bytes synchronously, populates the embedded viewport.Model via SetContent, and lands at scroll-tail.

Do:
- In internal/tui/pagepreview.go declare:
  - type previewModel struct { session string; enumerator TmuxEnumerator; reader ScrollbackReader; groups []tmux.WindowGroup; windowIdx, paneIdx int; viewport viewport.Model; width, height int }.
  - func NewPreviewModel(session string, enumerator TmuxEnumerator, reader ScrollbackReader, width, height int) (previewModel, bool).
- Inside the constructor, in order:
  1. Call enumerator.ListWindowsAndPanesInSession(session). On error, return (previewModel{}, false).
  2. If result is empty (len(groups) == 0) or len(groups[0].PaneIndices) == 0, return (previewModel{}, false).
  3. Set windowIdx = 0, paneIdx = 0. Construct viewport.New(width, height) (full terminal for v1; chrome layout is Phase 3).
  4. Compute paneKey := state.SanitizePaneKey(session, groups[0].WindowIndex, groups[0].PaneIndices[0]).
  5. Call reader.Tail(paneKey). Pass resulting bytes (which may be nil) verbatim to viewport.SetContent(string(bytes)). Do NOT translate (nil, nil) into placeholder text yet — Phase 4 owns wording.
  6. Call m.viewport.GotoBottom() so the first frame renders at scroll-tail. bubbles@v1.0.0/viewport.SetContent only auto-jumps to bottom when YOffset > len(lines)-1; a fresh viewport has YOffset == 0, so without an explicit GotoBottom() the user sees the OLDEST lines, contradicting spec § History Depth > Scroll within bounds and the Phase 4 task 4-3 test that asserts AtBottom() immediately post-construction. Tasks 3-2 / 3-3 already call GotoBottom() after focus-change reads — this preserves that invariant for the initial-open read.
  7. Return (model, true).
- Implement Update(msg tea.Msg) (previewModel, tea.Cmd) that delegates messages to embedded viewport. Cycle-key handling (], [, Tab) is Phase 3. Esc handling is task 2-4. WindowSizeMsg is task 2-6.
- Implement View() string that returns m.viewport.View(). Chrome rendering is Phase 3.
- Critical: bytes from reader.Tail must reach viewport.SetContent verbatim — no preprocessing, sanitisation, re-wrap, or escape stripping.
- Use state.SanitizePaneKey directly — its arguments must match daemon writer call site verbatim.

Acceptance Criteria:
- NewPreviewModel is constructor-injected — no package-level seam variable; both interfaces arrive as parameters.
- On enumeration error, NewPreviewModel returns ok=false and performs no further calls (no Tail, no SetContent).
- On empty enumeration (zero groups), ok=false.
- On a group with zero panes, ok=false.
- On enumeration success, windowIdx == 0 and paneIdx == 0 post-construction.
- reader.Tail is called exactly once during construction with paneKey produced by state.SanitizePaneKey(session, groups[0].WindowIndex, groups[0].PaneIndices[0]).
- If reader.Tail returns (nil, err) or (nil, nil), ok=true is still returned (preview opens).
- If reader.Tail returns (bytes, nil), those exact bytes are the argument to viewport.SetContent (no transformation).
- After SetContent, the viewport is at scroll-tail (m.viewport.AtBottom() returns true) — GotoBottom() is called explicitly because bubbles@v1.0.0/viewport.SetContent does not jump to bottom when YOffset == 0.
- Re-invoking NewPreviewModel for the same session triggers a fresh enumeration AND a fresh Tail call (no caching).

Tests:
- 'it returns ok=false when enumeration errors'.
- 'it returns ok=false on empty enumeration result'.
- 'it returns ok=false when first window has zero panes'.
- 'it sets focus to (0,0) on successful enumeration'.
- 'it reads tail-N for the (0,0) pane synchronously during construction'.
- 'it passes raw ANSI bytes verbatim to viewport.SetContent' — bytes containing \x1b[31mred\x1b[0m.
- 'it positions the viewport at scroll-tail on initial open' — fixture: bytes containing more lines than viewport height; assert m.viewport.AtBottom() == true immediately after construction; regression-pin against any future change that drops the explicit GotoBottom().
- 'it returns ok=true when Tail returns (nil, nil)'.
- 'it returns ok=true when Tail returns (nil, err)'.
- 'it constructs a fresh model per call with no carried state'.

Edge Cases:
- One group with empty PaneIndices treated as enumeration-empty for no-open.
- reader.Tail (nil, err) — preview must still open (Phase 4 will render error string).
- Bytes from Tail may be very large; SetContent accepts full slice without preprocessing.

Spec Reference: .workflows/session-scrollback-preview/specification/session-scrollback-preview/specification.md § Refresh Semantics > Initial-open ordering, § Multi-pane Rendering Shape > Model lifecycle, § Architecture Summary > Wiring shape.

title: Add pagePreview arm to page state machine and bind Space on Sessions page
description:
Problem: The TUI's page state machine has no preview arm and no Space binding. The spec mandates a new pagePreview arm peer to pageFileBrowser, with Space opening preview only from the Sessions page (Loading, Projects, FileBrowser pages must not bind it).

Solution: Add pagePreview to the page constant block in internal/tui/model.go, hold a *previewModel (or value-typed previewModel with a sentinel) on the root Model, route the page in the top-level Update switch, and bind Space in updateSessionList to construct a previewModel and transition to pagePreview when the constructor returns ok=true. When ok=false, the user remains on the Sessions page silently.

Outcome: Pressing Space on a highlighted session in the Sessions page transitions the TUI to the preview page with the tail bytes already loaded. Pressing Space on an empty Sessions list, with no highlighted item, while the list filter is in SettingFilter() mode (handled by passthrough — see task 2-5), or when enumeration fails, keeps the user on the Sessions page silently. Loading, Projects, and FileBrowser pages do not bind Space to preview.

Do:
- In internal/tui/model.go, add pagePreview to the page const block as a peer of pageFileBrowser.
- Add a preview previewModel field (and optionally a hasPreview bool sentinel) to the root Model.
- In the top-level Update switch, add a case for pagePreview that delegates to m.preview.Update(msg) and returns the updated model.
- In updateSessionList, add a tea.KeyMsg branch matching Space:
  1. If m.sessionList.SettingFilter() is true → fall through to bubbles/list's default handler (literal-space passthrough is task 2-5; this branch must NOT fire NewPreviewModel).
  2. If list is empty or m.sessionList.SelectedItem() == nil → return without transition (no-op).
  3. Resolve highlighted session name from the selected item.
  4. Construct previewModel := NewPreviewModel(sessionName, m.enumerator, m.reader, m.termWidth, m.termHeight). The seams m.enumerator and m.reader arrive from task 2-7's TUI construction; in this task the fields are added with placeholder zero values acceptable for compilation. The root Model already caches terminal dimensions in termWidth / termHeight — see internal/tui/model.go line 174 and the tea.WindowSizeMsg branch at line 700.
  5. If ok==false → return without transition.
  6. If ok==true → store m.preview = pmodel, set m.activePage = pagePreview, return.
- Confirm Loading, Projects, and FileBrowser handlers do NOT bind Space to preview.

Acceptance Criteria:
- pagePreview is declared in the page const block in internal/tui/model.go.
- Top-level Update routes pagePreview to m.preview.Update.
- Space on Sessions page constructs previewModel and transitions to pagePreview when ok==true.
- Space is a no-op when Sessions list is empty.
- Space is a no-op when no item is highlighted (SelectedItem() == nil).
- Space is a no-op when NewPreviewModel returns ok==false (enumeration error or empty).
- Loading, Projects, and FileBrowser handlers do not invoke NewPreviewModel.
- When Space is pressed during SettingFilter(), this branch does not call NewPreviewModel.

Tests:
- 'it transitions to pagePreview on Space when a session is highlighted' — synthesise a tea.KeyMsg{Type: tea.KeySpace} (matches the runtime shape bubbletea produces for a standalone space keypress; see internal/ui/browser_test.go for the existing in-tree pattern), drive Update, assert m.activePage == pagePreview.
- 'it remains on Sessions page when Space is pressed on an empty list'.
- 'it remains on Sessions page when enumeration fails'.
- 'it remains on Sessions page when enumeration returns empty'.
- 'it does not bind Space on the Loading page' — drive Update with m.activePage == PageLoading and a Space KeyMsg.
- 'it does not bind Space on the Projects page' — same but m.activePage == PageProjects.
- 'it does not bind Space on the FileBrowser page' — same but m.activePage == pageFileBrowser.

Edge Cases:
- Enumeration failure must be treated identically to empty enumeration (silent no-open).
- SelectedItem() == nil happens when committed filter narrows list to zero matches.
- Seam fields on Model may be nil during early construction; no-op branches must come before any seam invocation.

Spec Reference: .workflows/session-scrollback-preview/specification/session-scrollback-preview/specification.md § Trigger and Entry Point, § Refresh Semantics > Initial-open ordering, § Architecture Summary > Page state machine.

title: Esc dismiss returns to Sessions list preserving cursor and filter state
description:
Problem: Within preview, Esc must return the user to the Sessions list with the cursor at the same position it was on when Space was pressed, and with filter state preserved per the Esc level tree (committed filter remains committed; no filter remains no filter; mid-typing-filter is a sessions-page concern handled by bubbles/list's default Esc behaviour, not preview's). The preview model owns one Esc level: dismiss preview.

Solution: In previewModel.Update, intercept Esc and return a marker (either via a returned tea.Cmd carrying a previewDismissedMsg, or by setting a flag the root model consults). The root model reacts by setting m.activePage = PageSessions. The bubbles/list model is left untouched across the open/dismiss round-trip — its cursor and filter state survive automatically because preview never mutates it.

Outcome: Esc while on pagePreview returns the user to the Sessions list. The list cursor is byte-identical to where it was when Space was pressed (verified by reading m.sessionList.Index() before and after). Committed filter state remains committed; no-filter remains no-filter. A second Esc (now on Sessions page with committed filter) clears filter via bubbles/list's default behaviour — preview doesn't need to do anything.

Do:
- In internal/tui/pagepreview.go, in previewModel.Update:
  - Match tea.KeyMsg with Type == tea.KeyEsc (or String() == 'esc').
  - Return a sentinel tea.Cmd that emits previewDismissedMsg{}.
- In internal/tui/model.go top-level Update, handle previewDismissedMsg:
  - Set m.activePage = PageSessions.
  - Do NOT mutate m.sessionList.
  - Optional: zero out m.preview to release viewport memory.
- Confirm bubbles/list cursor (m.sessionList.Index()) and filter state (m.sessionList.IsFiltered(), m.sessionList.FilterValue()) are not consulted/mutated during preview lifetime.
- Note: re-fetch on dismiss (for externally-killed sessions) is Phase 4 scope.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Esc while on pagePreview transitions back to PageSessions.
- After dismiss, m.sessionList.Index() equals value captured before Space.
- After dismiss, m.sessionList.FilterValue() equals value captured before Space (no filter case).
- After dismiss when filter was committed before Space, filter remains committed (m.sessionList.IsFiltered() == true and same FilterValue()).
- Subsequent Esc on Sessions page falls through to bubbles/list default Esc.
- Re-opening preview after dismiss constructs a fresh previewModel.

Tests:
- 'it dismisses preview on Esc and returns to PageSessions'.
- 'it preserves the list cursor across open/dismiss' — set list cursor to index 3, Space, Esc; assert m.sessionList.Index() == 3.
- 'it preserves the no-filter state across open/dismiss' — list with no filter, Space, Esc; assert m.sessionList.FilterValue() == ''.
- 'it preserves committed filter across open/dismiss'.
- 'a second Esc clears a committed filter via list default behaviour'.
- 'it constructs a fresh previewModel on re-open after dismiss'.

Edge Cases:
- bubbles/list distinguishes 'filter input mode' (SettingFilter()) from 'filter committed' — preview never opens during filter input mode.
- Cursor preservation invariant relies on preview NEVER calling m.sessionList.Select or any list mutator.

Spec Reference: .workflows/session-scrollback-preview/specification/session-scrollback-preview/specification.md § Esc Level Tree, § Trigger and Entry Point, § No In-preview Between-Session Stepping.

title: Filter-mode Space passthrough integration
description:
Problem: bubbles/list's filter input mode (SettingFilter() true) treats every keypress as text input — Space must insert a literal space into the filter, not open preview. The spec mandates 'default bubbles/list semantics' — no magic-Space, no second binding for 'open preview while filtering'. The user must commit the filter (Enter) before Space opens preview.

Solution: In updateSessionList, the Space branch added in task 2-3 must explicitly check m.sessionList.SettingFilter() and fall through to the bubbles/list default handler (passing the message via m.sessionList.Update(msg)) when true. Only after the filter is committed does Space invoke NewPreviewModel.

Outcome: Typing 'pigeon ' (with a space) into the filter input adds a literal space character — no preview is opened. After pressing Enter to commit, the highlighted match opens preview when Space is pressed. The filter input field accepts spaces transparently.

Do:
- In updateSessionList in internal/tui/model.go, ensure Space branch begins with:
  if m.sessionList.SettingFilter() {
      // Filter input mode: Space is text input — pass through to bubbles/list.
      var cmd tea.Cmd
      m.sessionList, cmd = m.sessionList.Update(msg)
      return m, cmd
  }
- Confirm exactly one Space keybinding in updateSessionList — no second binding for 'open while filtering'.
- Confirm after Enter commits filter, m.sessionList.SettingFilter() returns false on subsequent Space events.

Acceptance Criteria:
- When m.sessionList.SettingFilter() is true, Space passes through to m.sessionList.Update(msg) and is consumed by bubbles/list as text input.
- When m.sessionList.SettingFilter() is true, NewPreviewModel is NOT called.
- After Enter commits filter, Space opens preview on highlighted match.
- No second key binding exists for 'open preview while filtering' — exactly one Space branch in updateSessionList.
- A literal space character is observably present in m.sessionList.FilterValue() after typing Space during SettingFilter().

Tests:
- 'it inserts a literal space into the filter while SettingFilter' — type 'pigeon', then Space; assert m.sessionList.FilterValue() contains 'pigeon ' and NewPreviewModel not called.
- 'it does not open preview while SettingFilter' — drive Space; assert m.activePage != pagePreview.
- 'Space after Enter-commit opens preview on the highlighted match' — assert m.activePage == pagePreview.
- 'it does not register a second open-preview binding for filter mode' — assert path consumes message via m.sessionList.Update only.

Edge Cases:
- A space at the start of the filter — passthrough must still work; bubbles/list accepts leading spaces.
- A space typed when filter is empty — same passthrough, no preview.
- Enter on empty filter — bubbles/list's standard behaviour governs; preview must not interfere.

Spec Reference: .workflows/session-scrollback-preview/specification/session-scrollback-preview/specification.md § Filter Behaviour with Preview.

title: Viewport scroll keys and resize handling within preview
description:
Problem: Inside preview, scroll keys must scroll within the focused pane's loaded N-line slice, with scroll-up at the top silently no-opping (the tail-N slice is a hard top edge — no deeper history extend in v1). bubbles/viewport's DefaultKeyMap covers Up, Down, PgUp, PgDn, ctrl-u, ctrl-d, j, k natively (verifiable in bubbles@v1.0.0/viewport/keymap.go); Home and End are NOT in the default keymap and need explicit handling in preview's Update — calling m.viewport.GotoTop() / m.viewport.GotoBottom(). Window resize during preview must re-flow the viewport without triggering a fresh disk read. Drag-resize fires many events; none must incur tail-N read cost.

Solution: In previewModel.Update, intercept tea.KeyHome and tea.KeyEnd explicitly and call viewport's GotoTop/GotoBottom. Default-delegate other scroll keys to viewport.Model.Update. Intercept tea.WindowSizeMsg to update m.width/m.height and resize embedded viewport, but do NOT call m.reader.Tail.

Outcome: Pressing Down scrolls viewport content down by one line; End jumps to bottom; Home jumps to top; Up at top is silent no-op. tea.WindowSizeMsg reflows viewport at new dimensions; scroll offset preserved. Across many resize messages, m.reader.Tail is called exactly zero additional times beyond initial-open call.

Do:
- In previewModel.Update, after Esc branch and before cycle-key handlers, intercept tea.KeyHome and tea.KeyEnd explicitly:
    case msg.Type == tea.KeyHome:
        m.viewport.GotoTop()
        return m, nil
    case msg.Type == tea.KeyEnd:
        m.viewport.GotoBottom()
        return m, nil
- For remaining scroll keys (Up, Down, PgUp, PgDn, ctrl-u, ctrl-d, j, k), default-delegate tea.KeyMsg events to m.viewport, cmd = m.viewport.Update(msg). Viewport default keymap covers exactly these eight via PageDown, PageUp, HalfPageUp, HalfPageDown, Down, Up, Left, Right bindings.
- Add tea.WindowSizeMsg branch:
  - Update m.width = msg.Width, m.height = msg.Height.
  - Set m.viewport.Width = msg.Width and m.viewport.Height = msg.Height directly — bubbles@v1.0.0/viewport.Model exposes Width and Height as exported fields; there is no SetSize method.
  - Do NOT call m.reader.Tail.
  - Return updated model.
- Confirm by code review that m.reader.Tail is invoked only from NewPreviewModel.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Down scrolls viewport down by one line (YOffset increases).
- Up scrolls up by one line.
- Up at top of loaded slice (YOffset == 0) is silent no-op.
- Down at bottom is silent no-op.
- Home jumps to YOffset == 0 via preview-owned m.viewport.GotoTop() (the viewport library does not bind Home natively).
- End jumps to bottom of loaded slice via preview-owned m.viewport.GotoBottom() (the viewport library does not bind End natively).
- PgUp, PgDn, ctrl-u, ctrl-d, j, k all behave per bubbles/viewport defaults (no preview-specific overrides).
- tea.WindowSizeMsg resizes viewport without calling m.reader.Tail.
- 100 successive tea.WindowSizeMsg events trigger zero additional Tail calls beyond initial-open.
- Scroll offset preserved across tea.WindowSizeMsg.

Tests:
- 'it scrolls down on Down key'.
- 'it silently no-ops scroll-up at the top'.
- 'it silently no-ops scroll-down at the bottom' — synthesise tea.KeyMsg{Type: tea.KeyEnd} to jump to bottom, then drive Down.
- 'it jumps to top on Home via preview-owned binding' — scroll down with PgDn, then synthesise tea.KeyMsg{Type: tea.KeyHome}; exercises preview's own Home interception (m.viewport.GotoTop()).
- 'it jumps to bottom on End via preview-owned binding' — synthesise tea.KeyMsg{Type: tea.KeyEnd}; preview owns this via m.viewport.GotoBottom().
- 'it does NOT call Tail on WindowSizeMsg'.
- 'it does NOT call Tail across 100 successive WindowSizeMsg events'.
- 'it preserves scroll offset across resize'.

Edge Cases:
- Empty viewport content ((nil, nil) from Tail) — scroll keys must not error; YOffset stays at 0.
- Single-line content — scroll-down must be no-op.
- Resize to smaller dimensions where YOffset exceeds new max — bubbles/viewport's native resize handling clamps offset; preview must not interfere.

Spec Reference: .workflows/session-scrollback-preview/specification/session-scrollback-preview/specification.md § Within-preview Key Bindings, § History Depth, § Refresh Semantics > Read Trigger Events, § Interaction Shape > Layout.

title: Production adapters wired at TUI construction
description:
Problem: The seam interfaces declared in task 2-1 need concrete production adapters wiring *tmux.Client (for enumeration) and the Phase-1 tail-N helper (for scrollback reads) to satisfy them. stateDir must be resolved once at TUI startup via the existing internal/state paths helper and closed over inside the ScrollbackReader adapter so preview never has its own state-path resolution policy. Pane key derivation must use state.SanitizePaneKey(session, window_index, pane_index) matching daemon writer call site verbatim. Tests in pagepreview_test.go must NOT import tmuxtest.

Solution: Add a small adapter type implementing ScrollbackReader.Tail(paneKey) that closes over stateDir (resolved at construction) and calls state.TailScrollback(state.ScrollbackFile(stateDir, paneKey), 1000). Confirm *tmux.Client already satisfies TmuxEnumerator. Modify the TUI construction site to resolve stateDir, build the adapter, and pass both seams into the root Model.

Outcome: When TUI is started in production, root Model carries a real *tmux.Client as TmuxEnumerator and a scrollbackReaderAdapter{stateDir, n: 1000} as ScrollbackReader. Preview reads from real .bin files; structural enumeration runs against real tmux server. Tests live in internal/tui/pagepreview_test.go, build with mocks (no real adapters), and do not import tmuxtest. stateDir captured exactly once during TUI startup, stable for Portal process lifetime.

Do:
- In internal/tui (e.g. internal/tui/preview_adapter.go), add:
  type scrollbackReaderAdapter struct {
      stateDir string
      n        int // 1000 per spec
  }
  func (a scrollbackReaderAdapter) Tail(paneKey string) ([]byte, error) {
      path := state.ScrollbackFile(a.stateDir, paneKey)
      return state.TailScrollback(path, a.n)
  }
  Use Phase 1's actual exported name if different from TailScrollback.
- Confirm *tmux.Client already satisfies TmuxEnumerator via Phase 1 ListWindowsAndPanesInSession. Add compile-time assertion in non-test file: var _ TmuxEnumerator = (*tmux.Client)(nil).
- Define const previewTailLines = 1000 (or wherever build phase prefers).
- At TUI construction site (typically in internal/tui/model.go or cmd-layer call site):
  - Resolve stateDir via existing internal/state paths helper (same source daemon and bootstrap orchestrator use — review internal/state/paths.go).
  - Construct reader := scrollbackReaderAdapter{stateDir: stateDir, n: previewTailLines}.
  - Pass tmuxClient and reader into root Model (assigned to enumerator and reader fields from task 2-3).
  - stateDir resolution must happen exactly once per Portal process — confirm by code review.
- Create internal/tui/pagepreview_test.go if not already present. Confirm file does NOT import github.com/leeovery/portal/internal/tmuxtest.
- Confirm state.SanitizePaneKey arguments in previewModel match daemon writer call site verbatim — open internal/state/scrollback.go and verify.

Acceptance Criteria:
- scrollbackReaderAdapter exists in internal/tui and satisfies ScrollbackReader.
- scrollbackReaderAdapter.Tail resolves path via state.ScrollbackFile(stateDir, paneKey) and reads via state.TailScrollback(path, 1000).
- *tmux.Client satisfies TmuxEnumerator (compile-time assertion present).
- stateDir captured exactly once at TUI construction; no per-open re-resolution.
- previewTailLines == 1000 per spec.
- Pane key derivation in previewModel uses state.SanitizePaneKey with same argument shape as daemon's writer.
- internal/tui/pagepreview_test.go does not import tmuxtest (grep-verifiable).
- pagepreview_test.go uses t.Parallel() safely.
- Production wiring exercised by end-to-end manual smoke test: build portal, attach to real session, press Space, observe preview rendering content from disk.

Tests:
- 'scrollbackReaderAdapter satisfies the ScrollbackReader interface' — compile-time var _ ScrollbackReader = scrollbackReaderAdapter{}.
- '*tmux.Client satisfies the TmuxEnumerator interface' — compile-time assertion.
- 'scrollbackReaderAdapter.Tail returns bytes for a valid pane key' — temp stateDir with known .bin; assert bytes equal expected tail.
- 'scrollbackReaderAdapter.Tail returns (nil, nil) for missing .bin'.
- 'scrollbackReaderAdapter.Tail returns (nil, err) for permission denied'.
- 'pagepreview_test.go does not import tmuxtest' — grep test.
- 'stateDir is captured once at TUI construction' — code review.
- 'pane key matches daemon writer' — code review.

Edge Cases:
- stateDir resolution helper consults $PORTAL_STATE_DIR first, then falls back through $XDG_CONFIG_HOME/portal/state to $HOME/.config/portal/state (per internal/state/paths.go::Dir) — preview must use this exact helper so both preview and the daemon resolve to the same directory; otherwise preview reads from an empty dir.
- If state.TailScrollback name differs from Phase 1's actual name, use whatever Phase 1 landed.
- Compile-time assertion var _ TmuxEnumerator may live in any non-test file in internal/tui.

Spec Reference: .workflows/session-scrollback-preview/specification/session-scrollback-preview/specification.md § Cross-cutting Seams > State Package API Reuse, § Architecture Summary > Test seams, § Architecture Summary > Wiring shape.

title: Phase 3: Multi-pane cycling, chrome, and focus-change reads
Focus state and pane-key resolution helpers
description:
Problem: Phase 2 left previewModel.windowIdx / paneIdx as 0-based ordinal indices, but Phase 3 introduces three focus-mutating keys (], [, Tab) plus chrome rendering. Without small, well-named helpers for 'current window group', 'current raw pane index', 'current pane key' and 'is single-pane single-window', subsequent code will repeat arithmetic and conflate ordinals with raw tmux indices.

Solution: Add small, pure helpers on previewModel that produce: (a) current tmux.WindowGroup, (b) current raw (window_index, pane_index) pair, (c) current paneKey via state.SanitizePaneKey, (d) degenerate() predicate for single-window single-pane. These carry no I/O — read-only views over groups plus windowIdx/paneIdx. Become single source of truth for cycle handlers (3-2/3-3), read trigger glue, and chrome renderer (3-5).

Outcome: Focused pane's tmux raw indices, paneKey, and group structure accessible via named methods. Subsequent tasks invoke these helpers instead of indexing groups[m.windowIdx].PaneIndices[m.paneIdx] inline. degenerate() check is single boolean call.

Do:
- In internal/tui (alongside Phase 2 previewModel), add methods on *previewModel:
  - currentGroup() tmux.WindowGroup — returns m.groups[m.windowIdx].
  - currentRawIndices() (windowIndex, paneIndex int) — returns m.groups[m.windowIdx].WindowIndex and m.groups[m.windowIdx].PaneIndices[m.paneIdx].
  - currentPaneKey() string — calls state.SanitizePaneKey(m.session, windowIndex, paneIndex) using raw tmux indices.
  - degenerate() bool — true iff len(m.groups) == 1 && len(m.groups[0].PaneIndices) == 1.
- These helpers must use raw WindowIndex / PaneIndices[] from cached tmux.WindowGroup, never m.windowIdx / m.paneIdx directly.
- Do not change previewModel struct shape. These are pure derived views.
- Add unit tests asserting helpers' output for: (i) standard 0-indexed, (ii) base-index 1 with non-contiguous gaps 0,2,5, (iii) single-window single-pane.

Acceptance Criteria:
- currentGroup() returns m.groups[m.windowIdx] and is the only call site that reads m.groups[m.windowIdx] from cycle/chrome code.
- currentRawIndices() returns values stored in tmux.WindowGroup.WindowIndex and PaneIndices[m.paneIdx] — not m.windowIdx / m.paneIdx.
- currentPaneKey() produces a key byte-identical to the one daemon writes for that pane.
- degenerate() returns true only for len(groups) == 1 && len(groups[0].PaneIndices) == 1.
- All four helpers are pure (no I/O, no mutation, no goroutines).

Tests:
- 'currentGroup returns the cached WindowGroup at windowIdx'.
- 'currentRawIndices returns raw tmux WindowIndex and PaneIndices[paneIdx], not ordinals'.
- 'currentRawIndices handles non-contiguous window_index (0,2,5) and base-index-1 panes'.
- 'currentPaneKey matches state.SanitizePaneKey on raw indices for the same session'.
- 'degenerate returns true for single-window single-pane and false for 1x2, 2x1, and 2x2 shapes'.

Edge Cases:
- Non-contiguous tmux window_index values like 0,2,5 must produce correct pane key (using raw 5, not ordinal 2).
- pane-base-index 1 sessions must produce correct pane key (using raw 1, not ordinal 0).
- Pane-key derivation uses raw indices; chrome (3-5) uses ordinals — these helpers expose only the raw side.

Spec Reference: .workflows/session-scrollback-preview/specification/session-scrollback-preview/specification.md § Multi-pane Rendering Shape > Chrome Floor (Counter semantics), § Cross-cutting Seams > State Package API Reuse > Resolution chain.

title: Tab cycle: next pane within current window with wrap and re-read
description:
Problem: Multi-pane windows currently render only their first pane. Spec mandates Tab advance forward through panes within focused window, wrap last→first, trigger fresh ScrollbackReader.Tail of newly-focused pane, and reset scroll to tail. Single-pane windows must silently no-op.

Solution: Extend previewModel.Update (in pagePreview arm from Phase 2) to handle tea.KeyTab. Advance m.paneIdx mod len(currentGroup().PaneIndices), perform synchronous Tail(currentPaneKey()), apply three-shape result to viewport, GotoBottom() to reset scroll to tail. When degenerate() or window has only one pane, Tab consumed silently — no mutation, no read, no Cmd.

Outcome: With multi-pane window cached, Tab updates focus, re-reads .bin synchronously, scrolls viewport to tail. In single-pane contexts Tab is invisible. Mock asserts exactly one Tail call per Tab press.

Do:
- In internal/tui/model.go pagePreview Update arm, add tea.KeyMsg branch matching tea.KeyTab.
- Compute paneCount := len(m.currentGroup().PaneIndices). If paneCount <= 1, return m, nil silently.
- Otherwise: m.paneIdx = (m.paneIdx + 1) % paneCount.
- Call m.reader.Tail(m.currentPaneKey()) synchronously; pass (bytes, err) through Phase 2's dispatcher to viewport.SetContent.
- Call m.viewport.GotoBottom().
- Return m, nil (no tea.Cmd — read is synchronous).
- Tab branch must be intercepted BEFORE catch-all that forwards keys to m.viewport.Update so bubbles/viewport never sees Tab. Full precedence coverage in task 3-4.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Tab on window with N≥2 panes advances paneIdx by 1 mod N and triggers exactly one Tail call.
- Tab from last pane wraps to paneIdx = 0 (windowIdx unchanged).
- Tab in single-pane window or degenerate session is silent no-op: no mutation, zero Tail, no Cmd.
- After Tab triggering read, viewport reports AtBottom() true.
- windowIdx never modified by Tab.
- Reading is synchronous within Update; no tea.Cmd for read I/O.

Tests:
- 'Tab advances paneIdx by 1 within a multi-pane window'.
- 'Tab wraps from last pane back to pane 0 within the same window'.
- 'Tab in a single-pane window is a silent no-op (zero Tail calls)'.
- 'Tab in a single-window single-pane session is a silent no-op'.
- 'Tab triggers exactly one Tail call with the paneKey of the newly-focused pane'.
- 'Tab resets viewport scroll position to tail (GotoBottom called)'.
- 'Tab does not modify windowIdx'.

Edge Cases:
- Single-pane window: silent no-op — verify mock recorded zero calls.
- Last pane wraps to pane 0 within same window.
- Scroll reset: a fresh re-read each focus change means no 'stale buffer at offset 50' path.
- paneKey passed to Tail uses raw tmux pane index via currentPaneKey() (task 3-1).

Spec Reference: .workflows/session-scrollback-preview/specification/session-scrollback-preview/specification.md § Multi-pane Rendering Shape > Within-preview Key Bindings, § Refresh Semantics > Read Trigger Events, § Refresh Semantics > Scroll Position Resets on Focus Change.

title: Bracket cycles: next/previous window with pane-0 reset and re-read
description:
Problem: ] and [ move focus across windows in cached enumeration. Spec mandates wraparound in both directions, resetting paneIdx to 0 on every window cycle (per-window pane focus is intentionally not preserved), fresh ScrollbackReader.Tail read of new pane, scroll-to-tail, silent no-op in single-window sessions.

Solution: Add two more tea.KeyMsg branches to pagePreview Update arm matching ] and [. Each branch checks len(m.groups) > 1; advances or rewinds m.windowIdx mod len(m.groups); ALWAYS sets m.paneIdx = 0; performs synchronous Tail; resets viewport to tail. Single-window cases (regardless of pane count) silently no-op for both keys.

Outcome: ] walks forward through windows wrapping last→first; [ walks backward wrapping first→last. Both land on pane 0 of new window, render that pane's tail-N bytes, scroll to tail. Per-window scroll/focus state NOT retained.

Do:
- In internal/tui pagePreview Update arm, add tea.KeyMsg branches matching ] and [.
- Both branches: if len(m.groups) <= 1, return m, nil silently — no read, no mutation, no Cmd.
- For ]: m.windowIdx = (m.windowIdx + 1) % len(m.groups).
- For [: m.windowIdx = (m.windowIdx - 1 + len(m.groups)) % len(m.groups) (the + len(m.groups) makes Go's % yield non-negative when wrapping from 0).
- Both: set m.paneIdx = 0 unconditionally.
- Both: m.reader.Tail(m.currentPaneKey()) synchronously, dispatch result to viewport, m.viewport.GotoBottom().
- Both must be intercepted before viewport-passthrough catch-all.

Acceptance Criteria:
- ] advances windowIdx by 1 mod len(groups) and resets paneIdx to 0; one Tail call.
- [ rewinds windowIdx by 1 mod len(groups) (wrapping correctly for windowIdx == 0 → last) and resets paneIdx to 0; one Tail call.
- After window cycle, paneIdx is 0 even if non-zero before.
- Single-window session both ] and [ are silent no-ops: zero mutation, zero Tail, no Cmd — regardless of pane count in that window.
- After cycling, viewport at tail (AtBottom() true).
- Reading synchronous in Update; no tea.Cmd for read I/O.

Tests:
- ']  advances windowIdx by 1 and resets paneIdx to 0'.
- ']  wraps from last window back to window 0'.
- '[  rewinds windowIdx by 1 and resets paneIdx to 0'.
- '[  from window 0 wraps to last window'.
- ']  in a single-window session is a silent no-op even if window has multiple panes'.
- '[  in a single-window session is a silent no-op even if window has multiple panes'.
- 'window cycle resets paneIdx to 0 even when paneIdx was non-zero before'.
- 'window cycle triggers exactly one Tail call with the paneKey of pane 0 of the new window'.
- 'window cycle resets viewport scroll position to tail'.

Edge Cases:
- [ from windowIdx == 0 wraps to len(groups) - 1; the + len(groups) modulo guard prevents Go's % returning -1.
- Single-window two-pane session: both ] and [ are no-ops (windows iterate windows, panes iterate panes; one window has nothing to iterate).
- Re-cycling back to a previously-visited window does not restore prior paneIdx — pane 0 always.
- New pane's paneKey uses raw tmux pane index from groups[newWindowIdx].PaneIndices[0] via currentPaneKey().

Spec Reference: .workflows/session-scrollback-preview/specification/session-scrollback-preview/specification.md § Multi-pane Rendering Shape > Within-preview Key Bindings, § Multi-pane Rendering Shape > Pane focus on window cycle, § Refresh Semantics > Read Trigger Events, § Refresh Semantics > Scroll Position Resets on Focus Change.

title: Keymap precedence over embedded viewport
description:
Problem: Phase 2 forwards arbitrary keys to m.viewport.Update so bubbles/viewport defaults work. Tasks 3-2/3-3 wire Tab, ], [. If those keys fall through to viewport — even after preview handled them — viewport could double-handle (e.g. consume Tab as focus key in future bubbles/viewport revision). Spec carves out preview-owned keys: 'preview's binding wins inside the preview page'.

Solution: Audit and codify dispatch order in pagePreview Update arm: preview-owned keys (], [, Tab, Esc, Home, End) matched first and short-circuit with their own return; only unmatched-default branch forwards to m.viewport.Update.

Outcome: After Tab, ], [, Home, or End press, embedded viewport never sees the key in a way that double-handles it. Conversely, Up/Down/j/k/PgUp/PgDn and other viewport defaults still pass through and scroll loaded buffer.

Do:
